prompt: Motor slip increases as load decreases on an induction motor. At very light load, efficiency drops mainly because:

options.text: Fixed core and friction/windage losses become a larger fraction of input power
The motor converts all input to mechanical output with no losses
Power factor automatically rises to 1.0 at light load
Nameplate horsepower increases at light load

explanation: At low load, fixed losses remain while useful output falls, reducing efficiency; this is why severely oversized motors perform poorly at part load.

distractorExplanations.b: All real motors have losses; zero-loss operation is impossible.
distractorExplanations.c: Power factor typically decreases at light motor load.
distractorExplanations.d: Nameplate rating is fixed; it does not increase with load changes.

prompt: A VFD operating a pump at 80% speed compared with full speed reduces shaft power approximately by:

options.text: 20% following the linear affinity law
About 49% using the cube law (0.8³ ≈ 0.51 of full power)
0% because pumps always use full power
80% leaving only 20% of full power regardless of system curve

explanation: For centrifugal pumps on similar system curves, power varies roughly with speed cubed: (0.8)³ = 0.512, about 49% power reduction.

distractorExplanations.a: Linear 20% reduction applies to speed change, not power for centrifugal loads.
distractorExplanations.c: Pump power drops significantly with speed on typical system curves.
distractorExplanations.d: 80% power reduction would correspond to a much lower speed than 80%.

prompt: Which tariff component is most directly targeted by automatic load shedding of non-critical loads during peak intervals?

options.text: Water sewer fees
Property tax assessments
Monthly billing demand (kW) charges
Customer service fixed charge only

explanation: Load shedding during peak intervals lowers the facility's peak kW, directly reducing demand charges on many commercial tariffs.

distractorExplanations.d: Fixed customer charges typically do not vary with peak kW.
distractorExplanations.a: Water/sewer fees are unrelated to electric demand.
distractorExplanations.b: Property taxes are municipal levies, not utility tariff components.

prompt: An LED troffer retrofit specifies 4000K correlated color temperature (CCT). This parameter describes:

options.text: The fixture's power factor
The motor full-load amperage
The boiler stack temperature
The visual warmth or coolness appearance of the light source

explanation: CCT indicates whether light appears warm (lower K) or cool (higher K); 4000K is a neutral-cool office common choice.

distractorExplanations.a: Power factor is an electrical phase relationship metric.
distractorExplanations.b: FLA is a motor electrical specification.
distractorExplanations.c: Stack temperature relates to combustion efficiency.

prompt: Conducting a lighting-level audit before LED retrofit primarily ensures:

options.text: Maintained illuminance meets task requirements after wattage reduction
Motors are replaced with premium efficiency models
Boiler turndown ratios are optimized
Chiller refrigerant is reclaimed

explanation: Photometric audits verify foot-candle levels and uniformity so retrofits save energy without under-lighting tasks or violating code minima.

distractorExplanations.b: Motor replacement is an electrical/mechanical ECM, not a lighting audit outcome.
distractorExplanations.c: Boiler turndown is a thermal plant topic.

prompt: A plant installs power factor correction raising PF from 0.78 to 0.95 without changing production. Apparent demand in kVA at the meter will:

options.text: Double automatically
Decrease for the same real kW load
Increase because capacitors add real power
Stay exactly unchanged always

explanation: kVA = kW / PF; for constant real load, higher PF lowers apparent demand, which can reduce demand charges billed on kVA.

distractorExplanations.c: Capacitors supply reactive power locally; real kW stays similar while kVA drops.
distractorExplanations.d: PF correction changes the kW/kVA relationship.
distractorExplanations.a: Apparent demand decreases, not doubles, when PF improves.

prompt: Bi-level or task tuning in office LED systems saves energy by:

options.text: Converting AC motors to DC without controls
Increasing LPD above code maximums
Adjusting output to required illuminance rather than maintaining uniform over-lighting
Eliminating all lighting in occupied spaces

explanation: Task tuning sets light levels to needed foot-candles by zone, avoiding energy waste from uniform over-design.

distractorExplanations.d: Occupied spaces still require adequate illuminance.
distractorExplanations.a: Motor conversion is unrelated to lighting control strategies.
distractorExplanations.b: Code LPD limits cap installed power; tuning reduces operating power within compliance.

prompt: Which motor rewinding practice can reduce efficiency if not performed to modern standards?

options.text: Using manufacturer-specified varnish and wire for identical slot design
Balancing the rotor after rewind
Testing insulation resistance after rewind
Rewinding with smaller wire gauge or increased slot fill losses versus factory design

explanation: Improper rewinds can increase copper and stray losses, lowering efficiency below original nameplate performance.

distractorExplanations.a: Proper rewinds to OEM specs maintain efficiency.
distractorExplanations.b: Rotor balancing is good practice after rewind.

prompt: A facility replaces magnetic ballast T12 systems with electronic ballast T8 then LED. The largest single-step savings typically come from:

options.text: LED conversion with lower wattage and reduced ballast losses
Switching from T12 magnetic to T8 magnetic with no lamp change
Cleaning diffusers only without lamp changes
Painting walls darker to absorb light

explanation: LED retrofits cut system watts dramatically versus T12/T8 fluorescent by combining higher efficacy with integrated drivers and better optical control.

distractorExplanations.b: T8 with electronic ballasts saves versus T12 magnetic but less than LED.
distractorExplanations.c: Cleaning improves output slightly but not like a technology change.
distractorExplanations.d: Darker surfaces increase required light levels, increasing energy use.

prompt: Input kW to a motor is 90 kW and output shaft power is 81 kW. Motor efficiency is approximately:
explanation: Efficiency = output / input = 81 / 90 = 0.90 or 90%.

distractorExplanations.c: 81% would imply 73 kW output at 90 kW input.
distractorExplanations.a: 9% would reflect only losses, not efficiency.

prompt: Installing a VFD on an elevator motor without manufacturer approval is problematic because:

options.text: VFDs only work on lighting circuits
Elevators always operate at variable torque like fans
Elevators require specialized controls and safety codes not met by generic VFD retrofits
Elevators have no motors

explanation: Elevator systems integrate safety, regeneration, and proprietary controls; generic VFD application can violate code and safety requirements.

distractorExplanations.a: VFDs are for motor speed control, not general lighting.
distractorExplanations.b: Elevator duty cycles differ from variable-torque fan loads.

prompt: A demand response program pays customers for curtailing load during grid events. Which load is typically best suited for curtailment?

options.text: Life safety fire pump operating during an emergency
Critical hospital operating room HVAC without backup
Data center IT load with zero redundancy
Non-critical process loads or HVAC with thermal storage buffer

explanation: Flexible, non-critical loads or those with storage can be shed temporarily with minimal impact, making them ideal DR candidates.

distractorExplanations.a: Fire pumps must run during emergencies and cannot be curtailed.
distractorExplanations.b: Critical care HVAC requires continuous conditioning.
distractorExplanations.c: Unprotected critical IT loads cannot be shed without outage risk.

prompt: Lumen maintenance (L70) for an LED product indicates:

options.text: Estimated hours until light output falls to 70% of initial lumens
The fixture power factor at end of life
Motor insulation class temperature rise
Demand charge ratchet percentage

explanation: L70 life rating projects when LED output depreciates to 70% of initial, informing maintenance and relamping schedules.

distractorExplanations.b: Power factor is an electrical characteristic, not L70.

prompt: A capacitor bank on a heavily VFD-loaded bus without detuning reactors may cause:

options.text: Increased motor synchronous speed above nameplate indefinitely
Harmonic resonance and equipment damage risk
Guaranteed unity power factor with no side effects
Automatic removal of all harmonics

explanation: Capacitors can resonate with system inductance and VFD harmonics; detuned or filtered banks are required on harmonic-rich networks.

distractorExplanations.c: Uncoordinated capacitors can worsen distortion despite PF improvement.
distractorExplanations.d: Standard capacitors do not eliminate VFD harmonics.
distractorExplanations.a: Capacitors do not raise motor speed above synchronous speed.

prompt: Replacing incandescent exit signs with LED versions typically saves energy because:

options.text: LED exit signs eliminate all emergency illumination requirements
Incandescent signs have higher efficacy than LED
LED exit signs use far fewer watts for equivalent visibility 24/7
Incandescent exit signs operate only 1 hour per year

explanation: Exit signs run continuously; LED technology reduces watts per sign from roughly 20–40 W to 1–5 W with long life.

distractorExplanations.a: LED signs still must meet emergency visibility requirements.
distractorExplanations.b: Incandescent efficacy is far lower than LED for this application.

prompt: A motor management plan policy requiring NEMA Premium for all new motors above 5 hp primarily reduces:

options.text: Building roof insulation levels
Natural gas commodity prices
City water pressure
Long-term motor energy consumption across the installed base turnover

explanation: Premium efficiency standards on procurement lower losses as motors are replaced, compounding savings over motor life.

distractorExplanations.a: Roof insulation is envelope policy, not motor procurement.
distractorExplanations.b: Gas prices are market/regulatory, not set by motor specs.
distractorExplanations.c: Water pressure is unrelated to motor efficiency policy.

prompt: An energy manager compares connected load versus demand meter readings. Connected load often exceeds billed peak demand because:

options.text: Not all equipment runs simultaneously at nameplate load
Meters always read double actual demand
Nameplate ratings equal actual operating load always
Demand charges use kWh not kW

explanation: Diversity factor reflects that equipment operates at partial load and not all at once, so peak demand is below sum of nameplate connected kW.

distractorExplanations.b: Meters are calibrated; they do not systematically double demand.
distractorExplanations.c: Actual load rarely equals nameplate on all devices simultaneously.
distractorExplanations.d: Demand charges are based on kW (or kVA), distinct from kWh energy.

prompt: High-bay LED retrofits in warehouses often include aisle lighting controls that:

options.text: Disable emergency egress lighting
Dim or switch fixtures in unoccupied aisles while maintaining safety minimums
Eliminate all lighting in occupied aisles during picking
Increase wattage when forklifts are absent

explanation: Aisle-level occupancy or motion controls reduce burn hours and power in low-traffic zones while keeping code-compliant minimum illumination.

distractorExplanations.c: Active picking aisles require adequate task lighting.
distractorExplanations.d: Controls reduce, not increase, power when areas are vacant.
distractorExplanations.a: Egress lighting must remain code-compliant and cannot be disabled.

prompt: Which measurement best verifies VFD savings on a fan compared with baseline damper control?

options.text: Number of lighting fixtures in the fan room
Boiler stack O₂ reading
kW draw at comparable airflow or static pressure setpoints
Motor nameplate voltage only

explanation: Savings verification compares input power at matched duty points (flow/pressure), isolating control strategy effects.

distractorExplanations.d: Nameplate voltage does not quantify power savings.
distractorExplanations.a: Lighting counts are unrelated to fan power measurement.
distractorExplanations.b: Boiler combustion metrics do not verify fan VFD performance.

prompt: A lighting retrofit maintains the same luminaire count but reduces watts from 32W to 15W per lamp across 500 fixtures operating 3,000 h/yr. Approximate annual kWh savings:

options.text: 0 kWh because lamp count is unchanged

explanation: Savings per fixture = 17 W; total = 17 × 500 × 3,000 h / 1000 = 25,500 kWh/year.

distractorExplanations.a: This overstates savings by a factor of ten.
distractorExplanations.b: This understates savings by a factor of ten.
distractorExplanations.c: Wattage reduction saves energy even when fixture count is constant.

prompt: Across-the-line motor starting draws high inrush current primarily causing:

options.text: Short-duration demand spikes and voltage dip on weak feeders
Permanent increase in motor nameplate efficiency
Automatic elimination of power factor penalties forever
Reduced need for any maintenance

explanation: Inrush can be several times full-load current, contributing to peak demand charges and voltage sag that affects other equipment.

distractorExplanations.b: Starting method does not change nameplate efficiency class.
distractorExplanations.c: PF penalties depend on overall plant power factor, not starting alone.
distractorExplanations.d: Starting stress may increase maintenance needs on mechanical systems.

prompt: Specifying luminaire efficacy (lm/W) in a retrofit RFP ensures bidders provide:

options.text: Fixtures without any photometric data
Efficient products delivering required lumens with lower connected wattage
Motors with highest slip at full load
Highest wattage fixtures regardless of output

explanation: Efficacy requirements tie light output to power input, preventing low-performance high-watt products from winning on price alone.

distractorExplanations.c: Motor slip is unrelated to lighting specifications.
distractorExplanations.d: High wattage without lumens wastes energy.
distractorExplanations.a: Photometric data is essential for compliant bids.

prompt: A facility uses kVA demand billing. Power factor correction is especially valuable because:

options.text: It replaces all motor maintenance
It eliminates energy kWh charges entirely
Reducing reactive power lowers billed apparent demand directly
It increases real kW consumption for the same output

explanation: On kVA-based tariffs, improving PF reduces apparent demand for the same real work, cutting demand charges more than on kW-only billing.

distractorExplanations.d: Real kW for the same mechanical output should remain similar.
distractorExplanations.a: PF correction does not affect motor maintenance schedules.
distractorExplanations.b: Energy kWh charges still apply based on consumption.

prompt: Group relamping versus spot relamping in fluorescent systems historically affected maintenance cost; with LED long-life systems, the best practice shifts toward:

options.text: Scheduled full relamping every 500 hours regardless of technology
Never inspecting fixtures because LED never fails
Replacing drivers daily
Preventive inspection and spot replacement based on failure or lumen depreciation monitoring

explanation: LED systems have longer useful life; condition-based maintenance reduces unnecessary group relamping while monitoring for driver or lumen failures.

distractorExplanations.a: 500-hour group relamping reflects old lamp life, not LED economics.
distractorExplanations.b: LED drivers and arrays can fail and require maintenance.
distractorExplanations.c: Daily driver replacement is unnecessary and costly.

prompt: An engineer sizes a VFD for a 50 hp fan motor. Minimum VFD ampacity should generally be based on:

options.text: Motor full-load current and manufacturer/heating requirements, including overload capability
Lighting circuit breaker size in the office panel
Boiler fuel input BTU/h only
Random undersizing to lowest price without data

explanation: VFD sizing follows motor FLA, service factor, and application duty; undersizing causes nuisance trips and overheating.

distractorExplanations.b: Office lighting breakers are unrelated to motor VFD sizing.
distractorExplanations.c: Boiler BTU input does not size fan VFDs.
distractorExplanations.d: Undersizing without engineering analysis risks failure and voids warranties.
